hamsterballin.title: Hamsterballin’
hamsterballin.subtitle: Roulette Runway · Tunnel owner
hamsterballin.cardDescription: A high-speed racing level built around readable risk, visual momentum, and confident player flow.
hamsterballin.heroDescription: Roll and bounce around as a cute hamster racing through imaginative tracks in this competitive arcade racer.

description.lead: Roll and bounce around as a cute hamster racing through imaginative tracks in this competitive arcade racer!
paragraphs: Master the control of your hamsterball to disrupt other players and find new, creative shortcuts and paths.

goals.title: Easy to Pick Up
goals.body: Use clear track layouts, readable lane transitions, and forgiving safety nets so players can quickly understand the movement system and feel comfortable experimenting with it.

goals.title: Fit the Gameplay
goals.body: Design the track around the game’s fast-paced racing experience, using elevation changes, tilted surfaces, and tunnels to make movement feel dynamic while supporting the overall gameplay flow.

goals.title: Reward Skillful Play
goals.body: Provide multiple routes and opportunities for precise lane switching, allowing experienced players to maintain momentum, take risks, and gain an advantage through better timing and control.

details.text: My initial idea was to let players switch lanes while running through the track, so I designed each lane at a different height.
However, during playtesting, I realized that switching lanes was too risky. Players often fell off the track, which discouraged them from experimenting with different routes.
To address this, I added a safety net beneath the track, allowing players to continue the race even after making a mistake.

Inspired by Counter-Strike’s Surf mode, I further refined the track design by tilting the surface and transforming the platform beneath it into a tunnel. This made the movement feel more dynamic while fitting the game’s overall theme better.
After many rounds of refinement, here is the final result.

postmortem.title: What Went Well
items: The project developed through continuous iteration, with each playtest helping us improve the gameplay experience.
Early playtesting helped us identify major issues and make meaningful changes before finalizing the design.

postmortem.title: What Went Wrong
items: Some mechanics were initially designed based on assumptions rather than enough player testing.
The project scope and time limitations made it difficult to polish every aspect of the game equally.

postmortem.title: What I Learned
items: Playtesting should happen early and repeatedly throughout the development process.
Design decisions should be based on both creative goals and actual player behavior.

firefly.title: Starfield: Firefly
firefly.subtitle: Modular medical lab
firefly.cardDescription: A circular combat space that guides exploration through story, light, and deliberate encounter rhythm.
firefly.heroDescription: A standalone Starfield quest where a delivery mission becomes a rescue operation inside a hospital under attack.

description.lead: Firefly is a standalone single-player quest for Starfield, designed for players around levels 13–17.
paragraphs: What begins as a delivery mission to Doctor Anderson quickly turns into a rescue mission when the hospital comes under attack. After securing the lab, players uncover the truth behind the experiments and must choose between sacrificing lives for a potential cure or shutting the project down.

The quest blends combat and storytelling across six combat zones, from an outdoor plaza to an indoor medical lab. Guided by the concept of circular flow, the level encourages players to adapt their tactics while narrative moments provide pacing and deepen immersion.

goals.title: Circular Combat Flow
goals.body: Design combat spaces with multiple routes and no dead ends, encouraging constant movement and giving players more tactical options.

goals.title: Landmark Design
goals.body: Use distinct visual landmarks to help players understand their location and navigate the level with less confusion.

goals.title: Hybrid Narrative
goals.body: Deliver the main story through quest dialogue while using terminals, notes, and scene composition to reward exploration and reveal additional story elements.

details.text: Circular Combat Flow

For the exterior, I used catwalks and high ground to divide the space into distinct functions: the main combat area, the main walkway, and an alternate path. I established the intended gameplay function for each space, then placed cover to support the utility of those zones.

I first created the catwalk, which separates the area into three parts: the paths on both sides and the central area. Because the middle was too wide, I divided it into two paths and then placed cover to fill the combat zone. To complete the circular flow, I added a secondary staircase at the rear of the catwalks and joined the catwalks in the center, eliminating dead ends and supporting continuous player movement.

For the interior, emphasizing verticality expanded traversal choices and introduced greater complexity to NPC pathing and AI behavior. Multiple staircases create a vertical loop between two levels, allowing players to retreat to high ground for a tactical advantage or drop down to flank.
Landmark Design

In the exterior, I used soft visual guidance. The architectural skyline creates leading lines that draw the eye toward the main objective, ensuring that the mission-critical building remains the focal point of the environment.

details.alt: Firefly exterior landmark and leading lines

details.text: Inside the quest, I used the Center Hall as a landmark. It sits at the heart of the interior structure and players visit it multiple times, so I reinforced its visual identity with unique assets and a specialized color scheme.
Hybrid Narrative
I expanded the background through branching dialogue outside the main questline, clarifying the origins of the conflict and giving players deeper context.

As the mission moves indoors, Doctor Anderson is the only character available for dialogue. I used terminals and data slates to offer alternative perspectives on the events, increasing the number of narrative beats throughout the level.

postmortem.title: What Went Well
items: The level has a clear story structure and a plot twist that supports the overall player experience.
Feedback helped clarify how spatial layout affects combat. Circular combat flow made encounters more dynamic and engaging.
Sub-goals, clear sightlines, and early bottleneck identification created intuitive navigation and consistent player direction.

postmortem.title: What Went Wrong
items: Limited experience with 3D spatial design and Starfield’s metrics led to several large-scale layout revisions.
Repeated wall assets and color variations made the environment visually repetitive and weakened narrative support.
Too much time spent refining layout left insufficient time for NPC sandboxing, AI navigation, and overall polish.

postmortem.title: What I Learned
items: How dialogue and environmental storytelling can create narrative beats and control pacing.
How feedback exposes major layout problems and improves sightlines, navigation, and player flow through iteration.
How to learn a new engine quickly through documentation, wikis, and AI tools.

firefly.metaDescription: Level and narrative design work for Starfield: Firefly.

blacksite.title: Half-Life 2: Black Site
blacksite.subtitle: Lock-and-key progression
blacksite.cardDescription: A tightly paced security facility where routing, backtracking, and tactical pressure work as one system.
blacksite.heroDescription: A single-player escape level focused on cover-based combat, environmental puzzles, and a gradual transition from the Citadel to City 17.

description.lead: Half-Life 2: Black Site is a single-player escape level set between Direct Intervention and Lowlife in Half-Life 2: Episode One.
paragraphs: Captured by the Combine after the Citadel train accident, Gordon Freeman and Alyx Vance must escape a hidden facility by fighting through Combine forces and solving battery-based environmental puzzles. The level focuses on cover-based combat, progressive puzzle teaching, resource management, and a gradual visual transition from Citadel architecture to the streets of City 17.

goals.title: Combat Design
goals.body: Create balanced combat encounters through cover placement, enemy composition, and resource distribution. Combat intensity increases gradually while giving players multiple strategic options.

goals.title: Puzzle Progression
goals.body: Introduce the battery and one-way fence mechanics through clear, step-by-step teaching, then reinforce learning through repetition, variation, and combination.

goals.title: Narrative & Environment
goals.body: Guide the player through an escape narrative while using level progression to create direction and accomplishment. The visual style transitions from the industrial Citadel to City 17.

details.text: Good Combat Flow

Cover Placement: Covers in the level are carefully designed. Players can use these covers to break complicated combats into a series of one-on-one battles. This makes encounters easier for players to tackle and gives designers more control over the flow.

details.alt: Black Site combat encounter with cover placement

details.text: Verticality Combat Design: Some combat encounters take place across different heights, giving players varied combat experiences and changing how they read the space.
Battery Reuse Puzzle
Battery: Batteries are used to unlock doors. I put significant effort into the interaction between batteries and slots so that the action feels clear and satisfying.

Expanding the Depth: Some windows can be opened from the other side, allowing players to reuse batteries from a previous room. These connections must be carefully designed so the puzzles remain fun to play without becoming breakable.
Lighting and Guideline Aid
Lighting: Important supplies and interactive items are highlighted. Lighting attracts the player’s attention and helps them locate and collect what they need.
Wires: All locked doors are connected to the battery slot and indicator lights with wires. This makes it easier for players to identify the correct unlock point.

details.alt: Black Site wires connecting a door to its battery slot
Black Site locked door and battery indicator

postmortem.title: What Went Well
items: Milestones were completed on time and maintained a high level of quality.
Feedback from C34 students led to useful iterations and improvements.
The cover-based combat was fun, readable, and balanced.

postmortem.title: What Went Wrong
items: The 3D skybox was locked in an early phase and became difficult to revise.
Alyx occasionally lost track of the player and stopped moving.

postmortem.title: What I Would Improve
items: Build a more robust companion-following system.
Improve the battery slot before the prison gate.
Rebuild the 3D skybox with stronger visual composition.
Fix minor potential soft-lock situations.

blacksite.metaDescription: Level, puzzle, and combat design work for Half-Life 2: Black Site.

kill-the-makers.title: Kill the Makers
kill-the-makers.subtitle: 2D puzzle-platformer
kill-the-makers.cardDescription: A puzzle-platformer that teaches its rules through spatial composition, sound cues, and iteration.
kill-the-makers.heroDescription: A 2D puzzle-platformer about movement, combat, resource management, and mastering environmental interactions.

description.lead: Play as Skidmark the robot and escape from the evil lab.
paragraphs: As you proceed through the levels, you need to kill the scientists and guards to regain your energy lost over time. With a precise hit of a switch, you decide whether a laser gate is an impassable barrier or your next steppingstone.

goals.title: Progressive Tutorialization
goals.body: Gradually introduce jumping, double jumping, dashing, and combat so players can learn each mechanic clearly before using them together in more challenging situations.

goals.title: Combat & Resource Management
goals.body: Teach players that combat is connected to survival by introducing enemies with different levels of aggression and showing that defeating enemies allows players to recover lost energy.

goals.title: Environmental Interaction & Mastery
goals.body: Introduce switches, laser gates, and environmental hazards through clear visual communication, then progressively combine these elements to build a sense of mastery.

details.text: Level 1
In the first level of the game, I follow Nintendo’s 4-step level design philosophy: Introduction, Development, Twist, and Conclusion.
First, I placed the camera center-left of the main character to lead the player’s movement. I also designed a small stair to encourage the player to press A and jump onto it.

details.alt: Kill the Makers Level 1 movement tutorial

details.text: The first enemy is the Scientist. Scientists run away when the player approaches, but they cannot harm the player. This creates a safe target for teaching how to defeat enemies and recharge the battery. I used a wall to stop the enemy from running away and make it easier for the player to kill it.
After players become familiar with the normal jump, I introduce the double jump to make learning feel more rewarding and give them a sense of progress.

details.alt: Kill the Makers double jump tutorial

details.text: This is the first time acid is introduced. It is relatively safe because the gap only requires one jump to cross, while the double jump gives players enough distance to recover if needed.
At the end of the level, I teach the dash. The final gap combines basic movement, double jump, and dash as a test of the player’s skills.
Level 2
In the second level, I introduce a new interaction and a new enemy.

At the beginning of the level, I teach players how to interact with switches. To communicate the goal, I place a sign on the wall and position the switch directly in front of the gate. In the following section, players gradually master how to use switches.

details.alt: Kill the Makers switch and laser gate

details.text: This level also introduces a more aggressive enemy. The Guard chases and attacks the player, causing them to lose energy. This reinforces the skill of defeating enemies while adding pressure to the resource-management loop.

postmortem.title: What Went Well
items: Teamwork was strong. After brief conversations, everyone understood what they needed to do next.
The outcome was successful from both gameplay and art perspectives, and players enjoyed the game.
Playtesting after every sprint helped the team identify what to change and how to improve.

postmortem.title: What Went Wrong
items: File management became disorganized during the later stages of development because of time pressure.
Time management was inconsistent: some tasks were underestimated while others were overestimated.
The Scrum board did not always include every necessary task, which caused critical work to be missed.

postmortem.title: What I Learned
items: How to communicate effectively with artists, programmers, and other level designers on a small team.
How to turn level-design knowledge into playable spaces through practice and iteration.
How to evaluate tester feedback by understanding the reason behind advice before applying it.

kill-the-makers.metaDescription: Game, level, and audio design work for Kill the Makers.

pit-stop.title: Half-Life 2: Pit Stop
pit-stop.subtitle: Encounter design
pit-stop.cardDescription: A compact action level focused on encounter staging, readable cover, and purposeful movement.
pit-stop.metaDescription: A level design project by Ruichi Li.

about.headline: Hi! I’m Ruichi Li.
paragraphs: I’m currently studying at SMU Guildhall for a master’s degree, specializing in Level Design. As a student who studies video games, I love playing games.
I like to play games on different devices, including arcade machines, VR, and especially all kinds of handhelds.
about.handheldAlt: Ruichi Li’s collection of handheld game devices
about.caption: Arcade, VR, and handhelds — every platform offers a different way to play.
about.racingText: I’m a Formula 1 fan, and my favorite team is Ferrari. Forza Ferrari! I also love all kinds of racing games, from Need for Speed to Assetto Corsa. Actually, the first game in my life was Need for Speed: Hot Pursuit 2.
about.esportsText: Esports is another major hobby of mine. I enjoy watching various esports tournaments, especially Counter-Strike and Valorant. I play both games myself, though my rank isn’t particularly high.
about.closeText: If you are interested in me, please contact me via email. I would be happy to hear from you.
about.metaDescription: Learn more about Ruichi Li, level designer and player.

resume.name: Ruichi Li

entries.title: Hamsterballin’
paragraphs: Level Designer, specifically owning the Tunnel in Roulette Runway.

Strategically placed and balanced all level pickups and interactive hazards, utilizing item placement to naturally guide player sightlines, reward high-risk maneuvers, and control the pacing of the race.

Executed rapid level iterations by analyzing playtest telemetry and user feedback. Collaborated within a cross-disciplinary team of artists and programmers under the Guildhall Development Methodology, utilizing Perforce (P4V) for asset pipeline management and Jira to track sprints and meet tight milestones.

entries.title: Kill the Makers
entries.strong: Game Designer, Level Designer specifically owning Levels 1, 2 and 3, Audio Designer.
paragraphs: Architected 2D puzzle-platformer layouts, strategically placing hazards and platforms to onboard players and teach core mechanics; integrated custom SFX via Adobe Audition to provide critical audio feedback for puzzles.
Collaborated to rapidly iterate on physics and jump metrics, utilizing playtest data to fine-tune character controls, platform spacing, and difficulty curves.

entries.title: Starfield custom level: Firefly
paragraphs: Architected a modular medical lab environment within the Starfield Creation Kit, applying advanced 3D layout techniques to establish a circular combat flow and optimize player navigation.
Programmed scripted events, NPC behaviors, and narrative terminal entries, leveraging the engine’s internal quest logic to deliver seamless environmental storytelling and immersive world-building.

entries.title: Half-Life 2 custom level: Black Site
paragraphs: Designed structured level routing and progression loops within the Hammer Editor, implementing lock-and-key gating puzzles that challenge players to navigate, backtrack, and unlock restricted sectors.

Programmed robust mission logic and encounter sequencing using Hammer’s I/O entity system, scripting custom triggers, door/gate interactions, and Combine AI tactical spawns to match original pacing standards.

entries.title: SMU Guildhall
entries.strong: Master of Interactive Technology, Specialization in Level Design

skills: Unreal Engine 5, Starfield: Creation Kit, Source Engine: Hammer, Unity
Whitebox-to-Shipped Level Pipeline, Spatial Composition & Sightlines, Environmental Storytelling, Pacing & Combat Encounters, Player Flow Design
C, C++, C#, Java, Python, 3dsMax, Illustrator, Miro, Canva, Perforce, Jira
Native in Mandarin, Professionally Fluent in English, Rapid Prototyping & Iteration, Avid player of tactical shooters and racing games
resume.metaDescription: Resume of Ruichi Li, Level Designer.

contact.title: Get in Touch!
contact.description: I’m always open to discussing new opportunities, creative projects, or interesting ideas. Feel free to use the form or connect with me on social media.
